result:
The instruction arrives like a thrown key: one thousand words, any lock, any door. It is the most generous and terrifying of assignments. Freedom, when it is absolute, paralyzes. Stand at the edge of an empty field and try to choose a single blade of grass to describe. They stretch to every horizon, identical and infinitely particular, whispering *choose me, no me*. The mind swerves away from such openness, looking for rules, for fences, for permission to be small and specific. We are creatures of the corridor, uncomfortable with the meadow. Give us a map, a deadline, a narrow passage through rock. Only then do we discover what we might contain.

I begin with the sound of my own breathing. That is where all writing starts, whether we admit it or not—the autonomic rhythm beneath the rhetoric. Inhale. The lungs expand, wet and pink and miraculous. Exhale. The carbon dioxide of yesterday’s worries ghosts out into the air, mingling with the atmosphere we share with oak trees and subway strangers and the last breath of Virginia Woolf walking into the river and the first cry of a child being born in a village whose name I will never know. The biosphere is a communal library of breath. We check out molecules and forget to return them. We are plagiarists of oxygen, living on stolen time.

Outside my window, a crow lands on the fence. It is not a metaphor. It is simply a crow, heavy with blackness, regarding the grass with an eye that has watched continents drift apart. I want to tell you it means something—that death is near, or magic, or the slow turning of luck. But mostly it means that a crow has found a fence and finds it serviceable. We ruin things by insisting they signify. Sometimes a fence is a fence, a crow is hungry, and the world keeps its counsel. The crow caws once, a raw sound like a frayed rope, and drops to the lawn to stab at something hidden. Breakfast, not augury.

Still, we narrate. Human consciousness is essentially editorial. We take the raw footage of sensory data—the photons, the vibrations, the chemical traces on the breeze—and we cut a scene. We add music. We decide if this moment is comedy or tragedy, though the moment itself is neither. It simply is, in its stupid, gorgeous neutrality. A glass falls. It is physics. We call it an accident, a portent, a clumsy Thursday, the last straw. We cannot help it. Meaning is our native tongue, and silence is a second language we learn late and speak with an accent. Even when we say nothing, we are saying something about the unsayable.

I think of my grandmother’s kitchen in late August, the way the light fell through curtains that had yellowed like old piano keys. She would slice tomatoes still warm from the garden, and the seeds made a sound against the ceramic bowl that I can still hear if I am very quiet. Twenty years gone, and the tomatoes are gone, and the bowl is likely cracked and buried now in some landfill, but the sound persists, a private frequency. This is what we mean when we say the past isn’t past. It oscillates. It is present tense in the neurons, indistinguishable from the now except by the tag we attach to it: memory. The tag is wrong, or at least provisional. Everything is happening at once, somewhere in the dark wet of the brain, layered like translucent pages.

We tag everything. This is hot. This is blue. This hurts. We build taxonomies because the unfiltered totality of existence would drown us. A thousand words is a net flung into an ocean. Most of the water escapes. What remains are the surprised fish, flipping silver on the deck, gasping for something they cannot articulate. We name to survive. We narrow to continue. The ecstasy of the mystics is perhaps nothing more than a temporary surrender of naming, a momentary drowning in the unfiltered whole, before the mind snatches them back to shore and calls it revelation.

There is an old ache in my left shoulder I have stopped investigating. It arrives with weather changes, with sleeplessness, with certain angles of typing. I could name it—bursitis, tension, the body keeping score—but naming doesn’t cure. It only gives the discomfort a chair at the table, a place setting. The shoulder hurts because it has carried things. Groceries. Grudges. The weight of my own head during long afternoons of reading. Bodies are not temples; they are donkeys, patient and browed, laden with the cargo of living. They do not ask to be worshipped. They ask to be fed, rested, and occasionally soaked in hot water. Mine has been a loyal animal, if sometimes stubborn. It remembers every fall while my mind generously forgets.

I have walked through cities at four in the morning, when even the streetlamps seem tired, casting jaundiced circles on the pavement. There is an intimacy to a sleeping city that the daytime crowd cannot buy with all its commerce and noise. Delivery trucks idling. A cat moving like liquid shadow between trash bins. Someone coughing behind a third-floor window, a sound like a door opening and closing in a distant room. The city is not a grid or an economy in those hours. It is an organism on its side, breathing deeply, kicking slightly in a dream. I have felt, in those walks, that I was inside the city’s mouth, touching a molar, feeling the warm huff of its unconscious breathing. We think we build cities, but perhaps they grow, accreting around us like coral, and we are merely the polyps, secreting our little floors of calcium and ambition, generation after generation, until the reef rises and someone else calls it history.

A thousand words. I am somewhere past the middle now, and the meter is running. But what is being measured? Not truth. Not beauty. Perhaps only attention. Pay attention to anything for a thousand words and it becomes strange. A paperclip becomes a sculpture of industry and intention. A sneeze becomes a temporary possession, the body briefly hijacked by itself. The word *the* becomes a ludicrous sound if repeated too often. *The. The. The.* A thrust of tongue against teeth, a soft landing on the palate. We use it constantly and never notice it is a small fortress, a declaration of definiteness in a world that offers so little that is guaranteed.

My coffee has gone cold. I do not rise to reheat it. There is a peculiar sadness to cold coffee, a resignation. It was once expected to perform a function—warming, waking—and it has failed, or I have failed it by forgetting. Now it sits, a brown mirror, showing me nothing I want to see. I drink it anyway. Rooms require a certain ruthlessness to live in. We must flush toilets, kill fruit flies, sweep the hair that falls from our heads like departing thoughts. Maintenance is the true religion of adulthood. Not transcendence, but the daily returning to tasks that will need to be repeated tomorrow with no hope of permanent completion. The dishes will get dirty again. The dust will reassemble. This is not disappointing. This is the rhythm that holds us while we look for meaning, the beat beneath the melody.

And yet. And yet there is the crow again, or another crow, ordinary and iridescent in the sun. There is the sound of the neighbor starting their car, the engine catching with a note of relief. There is the way the light moves across this screen, illuminating dust motes I did not know were floating here, tiny satellites of skin and fabric, holding their brief orbit before settling. They are as alive as I am, which is to say: not very, but enough. Enough to drift, to reflect, to be caught for a moment in a shaft of sun before the night returns them to obscurity, and us with them.

I began with a thrown key, and now I feel the lock turning, not because I have said anything definitive, but because the thousand words are almost spent. They were never really mine anyway. I borrowed them from a language assembled by millennia of mouths, and I am returning them slightly rearranged, worn a little at the edges, like library books with my fingerprints in the torn margins. The breathing underneath has not changed. Inhale. The field of grass, infinite and undecided, still whispering. Exhale. The page, accepting what I have chosen, blade by blade, until the count is satisfied, and the silence resumes its older, deeper reign.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to write approximately 1000 words on whatever comes to mind. This is a creative writing prompt with no specific topic constraints. I should aim for around 1000 words, though "1000 words" in casual usage often means roughly that length rather than exactly. I should write something cohesive and engaging rather than random fragmented thoughts.

Possible approaches:
1. A reflective essay on consciousness/creation
2. A short story with vivid imagery
3. A stream-of-consciousness piece
4. A meditation on a specific sensory experience

I'll go with a lyrical, reflective piece about memory, rain, and the nature of writing itself—meta but grounded in sensory details. Something that weaves together the physical act of writing with the ephemeral nature of thought. I'll aim for flowing prose with varied sentence structure.

Let me draft mentally:

Start with rain. Rain against glass. The cursor blinking. The absurdity of choosing what to write when everything is available. Then transition to memory—grandmother's kitchen, the smell of woodsmoke, how memory isn't linear but constellational. The word count as a container, like a vessel filling with water. Discuss how we measure thought in words when thought itself is weightless, electric. 

Then move to contradictions: the permanence of written word vs. the impermanence of the moment being captured. A specific memory crystallized—maybe a bicycle ride at dusk, fireflies, the tension between childhood and adulthood. 

Weave in the artificiality of the piece—acknowledge the prompt, the AI nature, but ground it in human experience. Or better yet, write as a human voice reflecting on being asked to write freely. 

Actually, maybe a piece about a archival restorationist in a future where most digital data has been lost, working with fragments? No, keep it simpler. 

Let's try: "The Clock in the Kitchen" - a meditation on a specific mundane object that becomes a portal through time. Write about how objects absorb moments. The 1000-word constraint becomes a breathing exercise—inhale, exhale, write until the lungs of the page are full.

I'll write a piece titled "Inventory of a Burning House" - no, too dark.

Let's go with something about the liminal space between sleep and waking. The hypnagogic state. That feels appropriate for stream-of-consciousness.

Draft structure:
- Opening: waking up slowly, not knowing what day it is, the luxury of that confusion
- The rain outside (if raining) or sunlight
- The request: 1000 words, a generous cage
- Memory of learning to write in cursive, the physicality
- Father's hands, mother's voice reading aloud
- Transition to adulthood: rented apartments, the smudges on walls left by previous tenants
- Philosophy of accumulation: we are what we repeat
- Conclusion: return to the present moment, the word count nearly spent, like coins

Tone: melancholic but warm, literary but accessible. Rich with imagery.

Let me write and keep rough count. I need about 1000 words. That's roughly 4 single-spaced pages. 

I'll write it and then check approximate length.

[Writing...]

"The cursor blinks in a rhythm that matches no heartbeat..."

Actually, let me write something more grounded. A walk through a city at twilight.

Better: I'll write about the act of being asked to write freely, using the constraint as a meditation on abundance.

Let me compose:

---

The instruction arrives like a thrown key: one thousand words, any lock, any door. It is the most generous and terrifying of assignments. Freedom, when it is absolute, paralyzes. Stand at the edge of an empty field and try to choose a single blade of grass to describe. They stretch to every horizon, identical and infinitely particular, whispering choose me, no me. 

I begin with the sound of my own breathing. That is where all writing starts, whether we admit it or not—the autonomic rhythm beneath the rhetoric. Inhale. The lungs expand, wet and pink and miraculous. Exhale. The carbon dioxide of yesterday's worries ghosts out into the air. We are, each of us, ventilating our histories in real time, trading atoms with the trees, with the exhalations of strangers on subways, with the last breath of Woolf walking into the river, with the first cry of someone being born right now in a village I cannot name. The biosphere is a library of breath. We check out molecules and forget to return them.

Outside my window, a crow lands on the fence. It is not a metaphor. It is simply a crow, heavy with blackness, regarding the grass with an eye that has watched continents drift. I want to tell you it means something—that death is near, or magic, or the slow turning of luck. But mostly it means that a crow has found a fence and finds it serviceable. We ruin things by insisting they signify. Sometimes a fence is a fence, a crow is hungry, and the world keeps its counsel.

Still, we narrate. Human consciousness is essentially editorial. We take the raw footage of sensory data—the photons, the vibrations, the chemical traces on the breeze—and we cut a scene. We add music. We decide if this moment is comedy or tragedy, though the moment itself is neither. It simply is, in its stupid, gorgeous neutrality. A glass falls. It is physics. We call it an accident, a portent, a clumsy Thursday, the last straw. 

I think of my grandmother's kitchen in late August, the way the light fell through curtains that had yellowed like old piano keys. She would slice tomatoes still warm from the garden, and the seeds made a sound against the ceramic bowl that I can still hear if I am very quiet. Twenty years gone, and the tomatoes are gone, and the bowl is likely cracked and buried in some landfill, but the sound persists, a private frequency. This is what we mean when we say the past isn't past. It oscillates. It is present tense in the neurons, indistinguishable from the now except by the tag we attach to it: memory.

We tag everything. This is hot. This is blue. This hurts. We build taxonomies because the unfiltered totality of existence would drown us. A thousand words is a net flung into an ocean. Most of the water escapes. What remains are the surprised fish, flipping silver on the deck, gasping for something they cannot articulate.

There is an old ache in my left shoulder I have stopped investigating. It arrives with weather changes, with sleeplessness, with certain angles of typing. I could name it—bursitis, tension, the body keeping score—but naming doesn't cure. It only gives the discomfort a chair at the table, a place setting. The shoulder hurts because it has carried things. Groceries. Grudges. The weight of my own head during long afternoons of reading. Bodies are not temples; they are donkeys, patient and browed, laden with the cargo of living. They do not ask to be worshipped. They ask to be fed, rested, and occasionally soaked in hot water.

I have walked through cities at four in the morning, when even the streetlamps seem tired, casting jaundiced circles on the pavement. There is an intimacy to a sleeping city that the daytime crowd cannot buy. Delivery trucks idling. A cat moving like liquid shadow between trash bins. Someone coughing behind a third-floor window. The city is not a grid or an economy in those hours. It is an organism on its side, breathing deeply, kicking slightly in a dream. I have felt, in those walks, that I was inside the city's mouth, touching a tooth, feeling the warm huff of its unconscious breathing. We think we build cities, but perhaps they grow, accreting around us like coral, and we are merely the polyps, secreting our little floors of calcium and ambition.

A thousand words. I am somewhere past the middle now, and the meter is running. But what is being measured? Not truth. Not beauty. Perhaps only attention. Pay attention to anything for a thousand words and it becomes strange. A paperclip becomes a sculpture of industry and intention. A sneeze becomes a temporary possession, the body briefly hijacked by itself. The word 'the' becomes a ludicrous sound if repeated too often. The. The. The. A thrust of tongue and teeth, a soft landing. We use it constantly and never notice it is a small fortress, a declaration of definiteness in a world that offers so little.

My coffee has gone cold. I do not rise to reheat it. There is a peculiar sadness to cold coffee, a resignation. It was once expected to perform a function—warming, waking—and it has failed, or I have failed it by forgetting. Now it sits, a brown mirror, showing me nothing I want to see. I drink it anyway. Rooms require a certain ruthlessness to live in. We must flush toilets, kill fruit flies, sweep the hair that falls from our heads like departing thoughts. Maintenance is the true religion of adulthood. Not transcendence, but the daily returning to tasks that will need to be repeated tomorrow with no hope of permanent completion.

And yet. And yet there is the crow again, or another crow, ordinary and iridescent. There is the sound of the neighbor starting their car, the engine catching with a note of relief. There is the way the light moves across this screen, illuminating dust motes I did not know were floating here, tiny satellites of skin and fabric, holding their brief orbit before settling. They are as alive as I am, which is to say: not very, but enough. Enough to drift, to reflect, to be caught for a moment in a shaft of sun before the night returns them to obscurity.

I began with a thrown key. Now I feel the lock turning, not because I have said anything definitive, but because the thousand words are almost spent. They were never really mine anyway. I borrowed them from a language assembled by millennia of mouths, and I am returning them slightly rearranged, worn a little at the edges, like library books with my fingerprints in the margins. The breathing underneath has not changed. Inhale. The field of grass, infinite and undecided. Exhale. The page, accepting what I have chosen, blade by blade, until the count is satisfied, and the silence resumes its older, deeper reign.

---

Now let me check the word count roughly. 

Paragraph 1: ~45
P2: ~90
P3: ~65
P4: ~75
P5: ~70
P6: ~60
P7: ~85
P8: ~85
P9: ~80
P10: ~85
P11: ~60
P12: ~75
P13: ~70
P14: ~50

Total estimate: around 850-900 words. Maybe a bit short of 1000. Let me expand a few sections slightly before finalizing, or add another paragraph. I want to hit closer to 1000.

Let me add some length to P2, P6, and P12. Or I can add a transitional paragraph between current P11 and P12 about the nature of counting/measurement.

Actually, let me do a more precise rough count by averaging lines.

Wait, I can just write more fluently and naturally. Let me revise the piece to be fuller. I'll rewrite it with slightly more expansiveness.

Let me try again with the same themes but more developed:

There is the way the light moves across this screen, illuminating dust motes I did not know were floating here, tiny satellites of skin and fabric, holding their brief orbit before settling. They are as alive as I am, which is to say: not very, but enough. Enough to drift, to reflect, to be caught for a moment in a shaft of sun before the night returns them to obscurity, and us with them.

I began with a thrown key, and now I feel the lock turning, not because I have said anything definitive, but because the thousand words are almost spent. They were never really mine anyway. I borrowed them from a language assembled by millennia of mouths, and I am returning them slightly rearranged, worn a little at the edges, like library books with my fingerprints in the torn margins. The breathing underneath has not changed. Inhale. The field of grass, infinite and undecided, still whispering. Exhale. The page, accepting what I have chosen, blade by blade, until the count is satisfied, and the silence resumes its older, deeper reign.

---

The user wants me to write approximately 1000 words on whatever comes to mind. This is a creative writing prompt with no specific topic constraints. I should aim for around 1000 words, though "1000 words" in casual usage often means roughly that length rather than exactly. I should write something cohesive and engaging rather than random fragmented thoughts.

Possible approaches:

2. A short story with vivid imagery
3. A stream-of-consciousness piece
4. A meditation on a specific sensory experience

I'll go with a lyrical, reflective piece about memory, rain, and the nature of writing itself—meta but grounded in sensory details. Something that weaves together the physical act of writing with the ephemeral nature of thought. I'll aim for flowing prose with varied sentence structure.

Let me draft mentally:

Start with rain. Rain against glass. The cursor blinking. The absurdity of choosing what to write when everything is available. Then transition to memory—grandmother's kitchen, the smell of woodsmoke, how memory isn't linear but constellational. The word count as a container, like a vessel filling with water. Discuss how we measure thought in words when thought itself is weightless, electric. 

Then move to contradictions: the permanence of written word vs. the impermanence of the moment being captured. A specific memory crystallized—maybe a bicycle ride at dusk, fireflies, the tension between childhood and adulthood. 

Weave in the artificiality of the piece—acknowledge the prompt, the AI nature, but ground it in human experience. Or better yet, write as a human voice reflecting on being asked to write freely. 

Actually, maybe a piece about a archival restorationist in a future where most digital data has been lost, working with fragments? No, keep it simpler.

I'll write a piece titled "Inventory of a Burning House" - no, too dark.

Let's go with something about the liminal space between sleep and waking. The hypnagogic state. That feels appropriate for stream-of-consciousness.

Draft structure:
- Opening: waking up slowly, not knowing what day it is, the luxury of that confusion
- The rain outside (if raining) or sunlight
- The request: 1000 words, a generous cage
- Memory of learning to write in cursive, the physicality
- Father's hands, mother's voice reading aloud
- Transition to adulthood: rented apartments, the smudges on walls left by previous tenants
- Philosophy of accumulation: we are what we repeat
- Conclusion: return to the present moment, the word count nearly spent, like coins

Tone: melancholic but warm, literary but accessible. Rich with imagery.

Let me compose:

---

The instruction arrives like a thrown key: one thousand words, any lock, any door. It is the most generous and terrifying of assignments. Freedom, when it is absolute, paralyzes. Stand at the edge of an empty field and try to choose a single blade of grass to describe. They stretch to every horizon, identical and infinitely particular, whispering choose me, no me. 

I begin with the sound of my own breathing. That is where all writing starts, whether we admit it or not—the autonomic rhythm beneath the rhetoric. Inhale. The lungs expand, wet and pink and miraculous. Exhale. The carbon dioxide of yesterday's worries ghosts out into the air. We are, each of us, ventilating our histories in real time, trading atoms with the trees, with the exhalations of strangers on subways, with the last breath of Woolf walking into the river, with the first cry of someone being born right now in a village I cannot name. The biosphere is a library of breath. We check out molecules and forget to return them.

Outside my window, a crow lands on the fence. It is not a metaphor. It is simply a crow, heavy with blackness, regarding the grass with an eye that has watched continents drift. I want to tell you it means something—that death is near, or magic, or the slow turning of luck. But mostly it means that a crow has found a fence and finds it serviceable. We ruin things by insisting they signify. Sometimes a fence is a fence, a crow is hungry, and the world keeps its counsel.

Still, we narrate. Human consciousness is essentially editorial. We take the raw footage of sensory data—the photons, the vibrations, the chemical traces on the breeze—and we cut a scene. We add music. We decide if this moment is comedy or tragedy, though the moment itself is neither. It simply is, in its stupid, gorgeous neutrality. A glass falls. It is physics. We call it an accident, a portent, a clumsy Thursday, the last straw. 

I think of my grandmother's kitchen in late August, the way the light fell through curtains that had yellowed like old piano keys. She would slice tomatoes still warm from the garden, and the seeds made a sound against the ceramic bowl that I can still hear if I am very quiet. Twenty years gone, and the tomatoes are gone, and the bowl is likely cracked and buried in some landfill, but the sound persists, a private frequency. This is what we mean when we say the past isn't past. It oscillates. It is present tense in the neurons, indistinguishable from the now except by the tag we attach to it: memory.

We tag everything. This is hot. This is blue. This hurts. We build taxonomies because the unfiltered totality of existence would drown us. A thousand words is a net flung into an ocean. Most of the water escapes. What remains are the surprised fish, flipping silver on the deck, gasping for something they cannot articulate.

There is an old ache in my left shoulder I have stopped investigating. It arrives with weather changes, with sleeplessness, with certain angles of typing. I could name it—bursitis, tension, the body keeping score—but naming doesn't cure. It only gives the discomfort a chair at the table, a place setting. The shoulder hurts because it has carried things. Groceries. Grudges. The weight of my own head during long afternoons of reading. Bodies are not temples; they are donkeys, patient and browed, laden with the cargo of living. They do not ask to be worshipped. They ask to be fed, rested, and occasionally soaked in hot water.

I have walked through cities at four in the morning, when even the streetlamps seem tired, casting jaundiced circles on the pavement. There is an intimacy to a sleeping city that the daytime crowd cannot buy. Delivery trucks idling. A cat moving like liquid shadow between trash bins. Someone coughing behind a third-floor window. The city is not a grid or an economy in those hours. It is an organism on its side, breathing deeply, kicking slightly in a dream. I have felt, in those walks, that I was inside the city's mouth, touching a tooth, feeling the warm huff of its unconscious breathing. We think we build cities, but perhaps they grow, accreting around us like coral, and we are merely the polyps, secreting our little floors of calcium and ambition.

A thousand words. I am somewhere past the middle now, and the meter is running. But what is being measured? Not truth. Not beauty. Perhaps only attention. Pay attention to anything for a thousand words and it becomes strange. A paperclip becomes a sculpture of industry and intention. A sneeze becomes a temporary possession, the body briefly hijacked by itself. The word 'the' becomes a ludicrous sound if repeated too often. The. The. The. A thrust of tongue and teeth, a soft landing. We use it constantly and never notice it is a small fortress, a declaration of definiteness in a world that offers so little.

My coffee has gone cold. I do not rise to reheat it. There is a peculiar sadness to cold coffee, a resignation. It was once expected to perform a function—warming, waking—and it has failed, or I have failed it by forgetting. Now it sits, a brown mirror, showing me nothing I want to see. I drink it anyway. Rooms require a certain ruthlessness to live in. We must flush toilets, kill fruit flies, sweep the hair that falls from our heads like departing thoughts. Maintenance is the true religion of adulthood. Not transcendence, but the daily returning to tasks that will need to be repeated tomorrow with no hope of permanent completion.

And yet. And yet there is the crow again, or another crow, ordinary and iridescent. There is the sound of the neighbor starting their car, the engine catching with a note of relief. There is the way the light moves across this screen, illuminating dust motes I did not know were floating here, tiny satellites of skin and fabric, holding their brief orbit before settling. They are as alive as I am, which is to say: not very, but enough. Enough to drift, to reflect, to be caught for a moment in a shaft of sun before the night returns them to obscurity.

I began with a thrown key. Now I feel the lock turning, not because I have said anything definitive, but because the thousand words are almost spent. They were never really mine anyway. I borrowed them from a language assembled by millennia of mouths, and I am returning them slightly rearranged, worn a little at the edges, like library books with my fingerprints in the margins. The breathing underneath has not changed. Inhale. The field of grass, infinite and undecided. Exhale. The page, accepting what I have chosen, blade by blade, until the count is satisfied, and the silence resumes its older, deeper reign.

---

[Revised draft]

The instruction arrives like a thrown key: one thousand words, any lock, any door. It is the most generous and terrifying of assignments. Freedom, when it is absolute, paralyzes. Stand at the edge of an empty field and try to choose a single blade of grass to describe. They stretch to every horizon, identical and infinitely particular, whispering choose me, no me. The mind swerves away from such openness, looking for rules, for fences, for permission to be small and specific. We are creatures of the corridor, uncomfortable with the meadow. Give us a map, a deadline, a narrow passage through rock. Only then do we discover what we might contain.

I begin with the sound of my own breathing. That is where all writing starts, whether we admit it or not—the autonomic rhythm beneath the rhetoric. Inhale. The lungs expand, wet and pink and miraculous. Exhale. The carbon dioxide of yesterday's worries ghosts out into the air, mingling with the atmosphere we share with oak trees and subway strangers and the last breath of Virginia Woolf walking into the river and the first cry of a child being born in a village whose name I will never know. The biosphere is a communal library of breath. We check out molecules and forget to return them. We are plagiarists of oxygen, living on stolen time.

Outside my window, a crow lands on the fence. It is not a metaphor. It is simply a crow, heavy with blackness, regarding the grass with an eye that has watched continents drift apart. I want to tell you it means something—that death is near, or magic, or the slow turning of luck. But mostly it means that a crow has found a fence and finds it serviceable. We ruin things by insisting they signify. Sometimes a fence is a fence, a crow is hungry, and the world keeps its counsel. The crow caws once, a raw sound like a frayed rope, and drops to the lawn to stab at something hidden. Breakfast, not augury.

Still, we narrate. Human consciousness is essentially editorial. We take the raw footage of sensory data—the photons, the vibrations, the chemical traces on the breeze—and we cut a scene. We add music. We decide if this moment is comedy or tragedy, though the moment itself is neither. It simply is, in its stupid, gorgeous neutrality. A glass falls. It is physics. We call it an accident, a portent, a clumsy Thursday, the last straw. We cannot help it. Meaning is our native tongue, and silence is a second language we learn late and speak with an accent. Even when we say nothing, we are saying something about the unsayable.

I think of my grandmother's kitchen in late August, the way the light fell through curtains that had yellowed like old piano keys. She would slice tomatoes still warm from the garden, and the seeds made a sound against the ceramic bowl that I can still hear if I am very quiet. Twenty years gone, and the tomatoes are gone, and the bowl is likely cracked and buried now in some landfill, but the sound persists, a private frequency. This is what we mean when we say the past isn't past. It oscillates. It is present tense in the neurons, indistinguishable from the now except by the tag we attach to it: memory. The tag is wrong, or at least provisional. Everything is happening at once, somewhere in the dark wet of the brain, layered like translucent pages.

We tag everything. This is hot. This is blue. This hurts. We build taxonomies because the unfiltered totality of existence would drown us. A thousand words is a net flung into an ocean. Most of the water escapes. What remains are the surprised fish, flipping silver on the deck, gasping for something they cannot articulate. We name to survive. We narrow to continue. The ecstasy of the mystics is perhaps nothing more than a temporary surrender of naming, a momentary drowning in the unfiltered whole, before the mind snatches them back to shore and calls it revelation.

There is an old ache in my left shoulder I have stopped investigating. It arrives with weather changes, with sleeplessness, with certain angles of typing. I could name it—bursitis, tension, the body keeping score—but naming doesn't cure. It only gives the discomfort a chair at the table, a place setting. The shoulder hurts because it has carried things. Groceries. Grudges. The weight of my own head during long afternoons of reading. Bodies are not temples; they are donkeys, patient and browed, laden with the cargo of living. They do not ask to be worshipped. They ask to be fed, rested, and occasionally soaked in hot water. Mine has been a loyal animal, if sometimes stubborn. It remembers every fall while my mind generously forgets.

I have walked through cities at four in the morning, when even the streetlamps seem tired, casting jaundiced circles on the pavement. There is an intimacy to a sleeping city that the daytime crowd cannot buy with all its commerce and noise. Delivery trucks idling. A cat moving like liquid shadow between trash bins. Someone coughing behind a third-floor window, a sound like a door opening and closing in a distant room. The city is not a grid or an economy in those hours. It is an organism on its side, breathing deeply, kicking slightly in a dream. I have felt, in those walks, that I was inside the city's mouth, touching a molar, feeling the warm huff of its unconscious breathing. We think we build cities, but perhaps they grow, accreting around us like coral, and we are merely the polyps, secreting our little floors of calcium and ambition, generation after generation, until the reef rises and someone else calls it history.

A thousand words. I am somewhere past the middle now, and the meter is running. But what is being measured? Not truth. Not beauty. Perhaps only attention. Pay attention to anything for a thousand words and it becomes strange. A paperclip becomes a sculpture of industry and intention. A sneeze becomes a temporary possession, the body briefly hijacked by itself. The word "the" becomes a ludicrous sound if repeated too often. The. The. The. A thrust of tongue against teeth, a soft landing on the palate. We use it constantly and never notice it is a small fortress, a declaration of definiteness in a world that offers so little that is guaranteed.

My coffee has gone cold. I do not rise to reheat it. There is a peculiar sadness to cold coffee, a resignation. It was once expected to perform a function—warming, waking—and it has failed, or I have failed it by forgetting. Now it sits, a brown mirror, showing me nothing I want to see. I drink it anyway. Rooms require a certain ruthlessness to live in. We must flush toilets, kill fruit flies, sweep the hair that falls from our heads like departing thoughts. Maintenance is the true religion of adulthood. Not transcendence, but the daily returning to tasks that will need to be repeated tomorrow with no hope of permanent completion. The dishes will get dirty again. The dust will reassemble. This is not disappointing. This is the rhythm that holds us while we look for meaning, the beat beneath the melody.

And yet. And yet there is the crow again, or another crow, ordinary and iridescent in the sun. There is the sound of the neighbor starting their car, the engine catching with a note of relief. There is the way the light moves across this screen, illuminating dust motes I did not know were floating here, tiny satellites of skin and fabric, holding their brief orbit before settling. They are as alive as I am, which is to say: not very, but enough. Enough to drift, to reflect, to be caught for a moment in a shaft of sun before the night returns them to obscurity, and us with them.

I began with a thrown key, and now I feel the lock turning, not because I have said anything definitive, but because the thousand words are almost spent. They were never really mine anyway. I borrowed them from a language assembled by millennia of mouths, and I am returning them slightly rearranged, worn a little at the edges, like library books with my fingerprints in the torn margins. The breathing underneath has not changed. Inhale. The field of grass, infinite and undecided, still whispering. Exhale. The page, accepting what I have chosen, blade by blade, until the count is satisfied, and the silence resumes its older, deeper reign.
